title: Accuracy vs. Size vs. Speed
concept: Practical deployment often means balancing a three-way tradeoff: higher accuracy may come with larger models and slower inference, while smaller faster models may give up some quality.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Which compression method is about storing model weights in lower precision?
exercises.explain: Correct. Compression changes model size and speed — which leads straight into the tradeoff question.

cards.title: You rarely get all three for free
cards.body: Teams often want the same model to be tiny, lightning-fast, and state-of-the-art accurate. In practice, pushing hard on one of those goals often pulls against the others, so deployment becomes a balancing act.

cards.title: The practical tradeoff triangle
cards.body: When people talk about accuracy vs. size vs. speed, they mean that a model with higher quality often needs more parameters and more computation, while a smaller faster model may lose some performance. Engineers choose a point on this triangle based on product needs, not on one metric alone.

cards.title: Pocket guide vs. encyclopedia
cards.body: An encyclopedia may contain more detail, but it is heavy and slow to search on the move. A pocket guide is lighter and faster, but it leaves some detail out. The right choice depends on whether you are studying at a desk or navigating in real time.

cards.title: Server model vs. keyboard suggestions
cards.body: A hospital server helping doctors may accept a larger slower model if the quality gain is valuable. But a phone keyboard suggesting the next word must respond almost instantly, so it usually needs a smaller faster model even if that means a small drop in accuracy.

cards.title: There is no universal best point
cards.body: A common myth is that one model can always be the best on every axis. Usually it cannot. The right answer depends on constraints like latency budgets, battery life, memory limits, serving cost, and how much accuracy the task truly needs.

cards.title: Accuracy-first vs. speed-first choices
cards.body: Different products land at different corners of the tradeoff triangle.

left.text:
Often larger models.
More compute and memory allowed.
Useful when every quality gain matters.

right.text:
Smaller, faster models.
Tighter memory and latency budgets.
Useful for phones, wearables, and live interactions.

exercises.prompt: A phone app must answer in under 50 milliseconds. Which priority is most important?

options.text: Low latency and a model small enough to run quickly on the phone.
Using the biggest model possible regardless of speed.
Ignoring deployment constraints because accuracy is the only metric.
Adding parameters until the app stops opening.

exercises.explain: Right. Real-time products often place strict limits on model size and response time.

exercises.prompt: True or false: The smallest fastest model is always the best choice, even if its predictions become much less useful.
exercises.explain: False. Speed and size matter, but the model still has to be accurate enough for the task.

exercises.sentence: Teams sometimes accept a small accuracy drop to get much lower ___.
exercises.explain: Latency is correct. Faster responses can matter a lot in user-facing systems.

exercises.prompt: Which note shows the tradeoff mindset best?

options.text: Our augmented-reality app can lose a little accuracy if that lets the model run smoothly in real time on the headset.
The only metric that ever matters is parameter count.
If a model is accurate in the lab, latency will never matter in production.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Deployment is about finding a useful balance, not blindly maximizing one number.

exercises.prompt: A translation feature must run offline on a phone with a tight battery budget. One model is slightly more accurate but much larger and slower. Another is a bit less accurate but runs comfortably within the speed budget. Which choice is usually more practical?

options.text: Choose the smaller faster model because the deployment constraints make responsiveness and battery use part of success.
Choose the larger slower model automatically because any accuracy gain beats every other concern.
Avoid using any model because tradeoffs mean deployment is impossible.
Judge only by parameter count without thinking about accuracy or speed.

exercises.explain: Exactly. A practical model is one that is accurate enough and still fits the device, latency, and power limits.
